title: Macrolobium ivorense
intro: Macrolobium ivorense, also known as African walnut, is a plant species native to tropical Africa, belonging to the family Fabaceae. The plant has been found to possess various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of fever, rheumatism, and respiratory infections. The bioactive constituents of Macrolobium ivorense include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic compounds, such as isoflavones and anthocyanins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is often prepared into herbal teas, infusions, or decoctions, as well as being used in the form of extracts or tinctures, to harness its therapeutic potential.
taxonomy: Macrolobium ivorense is classified within the family Fabaceae, which is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as legumes. This family is further divided into several subfamilies, and Macrolobium ivorense is specifically placed within the subfamily Faboideae. The genus Macrolobium is a group of tropical trees found in West Africa, and Macrolobium ivorense is one of the species within this genus. The classification of Macrolobium ivorense is based on its morphological characteristics and genetic relationships with other plants. Further research and study may be necessary to confirm the exact classification of Macrolobium ivorense.

In traditional medicine, Macrolobium ivorense is used to treat fever, wounds, and skin infections.
